content: Passwords in Thunderbird are stored (in Windows) at menu option <Tools | Settings | Privacy & Security | Passwords | Saved Passwords (button)>.
creator: rednoise

content:
To make sure that I understand: When you look at the contents of the all mail folder in Thunderbird (not in the file system), you see many messages, including those that you deleted. Those messages appear normal. Their rows in the message table are not, for example, struck through. There are few messages in the trash folder. When you look at the contents of the all mail folder in webmail at Proton, you see very few messages.

Is all that correct?

Standard actions to try in similar situations are first to make a back-up copy of your profile, then empty the trash (apparently, this step is required to get messages out of the all mail folder even if they have already been "deleted") and see if it makes a difference. Then compact folders and see if it makes a difference.

But if my understanding of your situation is correct, I do not know a reason why those actions would help. Something has caused Thunderbird and Proton to become out of sync. The only thing that I know enough to suspect is the Proton Mail Bridge app. Do you always run it when you use Thunderbird? You have to run it, don't you?

But you could do back-up, empty trash, and compact folders anyway and see if those actions make any difference.
creator: mozilla98

content:
Those "unconfirmed" files are temporary/incomplete files created by Chrome as you are downloading. If the download is interrupted or otherwise not able to complete, you may find the half-finished file left behind. It is useless on its own. If you try to download it again, you might get lucky and the download will resume and complete that file, but it's more likely that it will start all over again and the "unconfirmed" file will be abandoned, in which case you can just delete it.

A properly downloaded Windows installation file would be named "Thunderbird Setup xxx.x.exe" or similar.
creator: rednoise

content: It looks like the buttons you need are off-screen, at the bottom. Scroll down (with your mouse wheel, if necessary) and click Allow, if you want Thunderbird to handle your Gmail. If you don't want that, click Deny and remove your Gmail account from Thunderbird.

content:
I dug into Bitdefender website.

I was directed to the Protection menu and then to Advanced Threat Defense. Then I added an exception to the Thunderbird.exe. 

Problem solved.
